Xbox Shader Assembler Error Codes

Code Description
1 Unknown preprocessor directive. The source contains a line that starts with #, but isn't one of the known preprocessor directives. An example would be:

#myDirective blah

 
2 Error directive. The source contains an "#error" preprocessor directive. For example:

#error This file is obsolete.

 
3 Symbol expected. A symbol was expected, but was not found. For example:

#define + // error: + is not a symbol.

 
4 Symbol already defined. The source is trying to redefine an existing symbol. For example:

#define COLOR v2
#define COLOR v3 // Error: symbol COLOR is already defined

If you really want to do this, use #undef to undefine the symbol before redefining it:

#define COLOR v2
#undef COLOR
#define COLOR v3

 
5 Can't Open File. The source is trying to include a file that does not exist. For example:

#include "NoSuchFile.h" // Error if NoSuchFile.h does not exist
6 Too many levels of include. The source has used too many levels of nested includes. The maximum is 20 levels. One way this can happen is if a file accidentally includes itself:

// MyFile.h
#include "MyFile.h" // Error, infinite recursion
7 Missing Resolver. The source contains an "#include" statement, but the XGAssembleShader function was called with a NULL resolver. To fix this error either remove the #include statement, or supply a resolver.
8 File name is not quoted. The source contains an #include statement without double-quotes or angle-brackets around the file name. For example:

#include MyFile.h // Error - need double-quotes or angle brackets..
#include >MyFile.h< // Error - The angle brackets need to by in the other order.
#include "MyFile.h" // OK
#include <MyFile.h> // OK
9 File Name Missing. The source contains an #include statement, but no file name. For example:

#include

 
10 Include Name Too Long. The file path specified in the #include statement is too long. The maximum length is MAX_PATH characters long.
11 Expanded Macro Too Long. The fully expanded statement is too large to fit into available RAM.
12 Source Line Too Long. A line of the source is too large to fit into available RAM.
13 Expected Macro Name. The source had a "macro" statement, without a macro name:

macro // Error need to specify a macro name.
14 Expected endm. The source had a "macro" statement, but the file ended before an "endm" statement was seen.
15 Macro Argument Syntax Error. A "#define" statement was encountered, but the argument list had syntax errors. The argument list should start with a '(', contain one or more symbols separated by commas, and end with a ')'. For example:

#define MYDEFINE(X,Y,+)  // Error - '+' is not a valid macro argument name
16 Expected macro parameter. In a "macro" definition, a '%' character was encountered, but was not followed by a macro parameter name. For example:

macro MyMacro a,b
  mov r0,%c // Error - c is not a parameter of this macro.
endm
17 Too many levels of IF. This source file has too many levels of nested #if or #ifdef statements. The limit is 20 levels. Each file has its own limit, so when one file is included in another, each file can have up to 20 levels of nested "#ifdef" statements.
18 Unexpected "#else". The source file contains an "#else" statement without a preceding "#if", "#elif" or "#ifdef" statement.
19 Unexpected "#endif". The source file contains an "#endif" statement without a preceding "#if", "#else", "#elif" or "#ifdef" statement.
20 Expected "#endif". The source file contains an "#if" or "#ifdef" statement, but does not contain a corresponding "#endif" statement.
21 Don't Handle "#if". The source file contains an "#if" statement. "#if" statements are not currently handled. Consider using "#ifdef" instead.
22 Don't Handle "#elif". The source file contains an "#elif" statement. "#elif" statements are not currently handled. Consider using "#ifdef" instead.
23 Unterminated C-style comment. The file contains the start of a C-style comment, "/*", but does not contain the end of the C-style comment, "*/".
100 Warning: Unknown Pragma. The source file contains a #pragma statement with an unknown pragma type. This is not an error, but it usually indicates a misspelling of the pragma name. An example would be:

#pragma screenspace2 // error - intended to write screenspace
101 Unsupported Preprocessor Directive. The source file contains an unknown or unsupported preprocessor directive. This is similar to error 1, except that error 1 is generated by the preprocessor, while this error is generated by the assembler. One way to cause this error would be to disable the preprocessor, so that preprocessor directives are sent to the assembler.
102 Invalid Version. The shader source file is for a version number that is not supported by the shader assembler. An example would be:

vs.10.10 // Error, version 10.10 is not supported.
103 Complement Not Supported. The compliment operator is not supported in vertex shaders. It is only supported in pixel shaders.
104 Complement Cannot be Used with Other Modifiers. The compliment operator cannot be used with other modifiers.
105 Validation error. The validator has found a potential problem with your shader, such as reading from a temporary variable before it has been written to.
106 Syntax error. The syntax of the shader source is incorrect. This is a catch-all error that can be caused by many specific mistakes. Double-check the syntax of your source code near the line where the error was reported.
107 Illegal register in index expression. You can only use the index register, a0.x, in an index expression. For example:

mov r0, c[a0.y] // error, must be a0.x
mov r0, c[r0.x] // error, must be a0.x
108 Duplicate A0.x. A0.x can only appear once in an index expression. For example:

mov r0,c[a0.x+a0.x] // Illegal - a0 can only appear once.
109 Internal Assembler Error. The assembler has discovered that its internal state is inconsistent.
110 Invalid write mask. The specified mask is invalid. Examples

mov r0.s,r2 // Illegal - 's' is not a valid mask element.
111 Invalid swizzle. The specified swizzle is invalid. Example:

mov r0,r2.xyzs // Illegal - 's' is not a valid swizzle element.
112 Invalid register. The specified register is invalid for this type of shader. Example:

mov r0,r100 // Illegal, there is no register 100.
113 Unexpected destination modifier. Source modifiers are not allowed on destinations. Example:

mov -r0,r1 // Illegal, you can't negate the destination register.
mov r0,-r1 // OK - it's fine to negate a source register.

 
114 Invalid destination register. The specified destination register is invalid, probably because it cannot be written to. Example:

mov c[0],r0 // Invalid in a normal 'vs' style shader. This statement would be OK in a 'xvsw' shader.
115 Invalid write mask. For a pixel shader, the only valid masks are '.rgb', '.a', and '.rgba'
116 Invalid source register. The specified source register is invalid, because it cannot be read from. Example:

mov r0,oPos // Can't read from oPos
117 Invalid constant. A register other than a C register was specified for a 'def' instruction. For example:

def c0,0,0,0,0 // OK
def c[a0.x],0,0,0,0 // Error - can't use relative addressing in a def statement.
def r0,0,0,0,0 // Error - can only use 'c' registers in a def statement.
118 Expected version statement. The shader has to start with a version statement. This is typically 'xvs.1.1' for vertex shaders, and 'xps.1.1' for pixel shaders.
600 Too many instructions. You have specified too many instructions for a valid shader.
601 No temporary registers available. This error can occur in complicated vertex shaders when the source contains a "log", "exp", or "frc" macro instruction. These three instructions sometimes require the use of a temporary register. The assembler tries to find a temporary ('r') register that is not being used by your shader program. If no register can be found, this error is generated.
602 Warning: Unsupported Macro. In normal DirectX 8 the "log" and "exp" macro instructions are supposed to be more precise than the "logp" and "expp" primitive instructions. But on Xbox these two macro instructions are no more precise than their primitive versions. This warning is generated to remind you to consider changing your shader to use "logp" and "expp" directly.

See Also

XGAssembleShader, Xbox Shader Assembler Tool (xsasm)
